risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The randomization is performed in 1:1 equal allocation ratio. The 610 opaque, sealed, and sequentially numbered randomization envelopes are mixed and distributed to research hospitals by the main research center according to each hospital district population. To randomize a patient, an independent surgeon on duty will open the next consecutively numbered envelope." Comment: Although the exact method used to generate the sequence inside the envelopes is not spelled out, the use of sequentially numbered sealed envelopes prepared centrally and mixed/distributed for 1:1 allocation strongly indicates a proper random allocation process rather than a non-random or predictable one. Per the permissive criteria, this is sufficient to judge random sequence generation as low risk.

Quote: "The 610 opaque, sealed, and sequentially numbered randomization envelopes are mixed and distributed to research hospitals... To randomize a patient, an independent surgeon on duty will open the next consecutively numbered envelope." Comment: This explicitly describes a sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelope system (SNOSE), which prevents the recruiter from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The APPAC trial has been designed as a prospective randomized controlled, open label, non-inferiority multicenter trial to compare antibiotic therapy ... with emergency appendectomy" and "the concealment would not be possible and therefore a randomized open design was chosen." Comment: This was explicitly an open-label trial, so participants and treating personnel were not blinded. The study outcomes included symptoms, pain scores, duration of stay, complications, and treatment success, all of which could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation; therefore the dominant risk for performance bias is high.

Summary: The trial was explicitly "open label," so outcome assessors were not blinded. Outcome assessment was performed by a mix of surgeons/clinicians and patient phone interviews/self-report. The study states that "Patient outcome will be obtained during hospital stay ... and then by a phone interview" and that pain score (VAS), sick leave, wound infections, recurrent appendicitis, and complications were recorded; these include subjective or judgment-dependent outcomes (e.g., pain VAS, sick leave, clinical diagnosis of recurrent appendicitis, wound infection diagnosed by a surgeon). Comment: Because the study was open-label and included subjective/judgment-based outcomes assessed by patients or clinicians aware of treatment, knowledge of allocation could influence measurement of at least one outcome, so the domain is High risk.

Quote: "The main analyses will be based on the intention-to-treat principle, but both intention-to-treat and per-protocol analyses will be performed." Quote: "With an estimated 10 percent of the trial patients lost to follow-up, a maximum of 610 patients will be enrolled." Comment: This is a protocol paper and does not report actual attrition, withdrawals, exclusions, or per-arm follow-up numbers for the analysed outcomes. It only anticipates ~10% loss to follow-up and states that ITT and per-protocol analyses will be done. Because no realized attrition data are provided, the risk of bias from incomplete outcome data cannot be judged from the paper itself.
